description.summary: Futakuchi-onna is a Japanese yokai — a two-mouthed woman — whose hair moves like serpents to feed a second, fully functional mouth on the back of her head that screams demands for food and causes excruciating pain if unfed.
description.full: The Futakuchi-onna (二口女, 'two-mouthed woman') is a Japanese yokai featuring a woman with two mouths — a normal one on her face and a second one on the back of the head, hidden beneath the hair. The second mouth is fully functional, with lips, teeth, and a tongue. The creature's hair moves independently like a pair of serpents, allowing it to manipulate food and feed the back mouth. If the second mouth is not fed, it causes the woman tremendous pain and screams constantly — mumbling spiteful and threatening things. The condition arises through a curse, with three primary origin narratives: (1) A wife of a miser eats little and develops a second mouth that demands food, consuming double what a normal person would eat; (2) A stingy woman struck in the head by her husband's axe while chopping wood develops an unhealable wound that transforms into the second mouth; (3) A stepmother or step-sibling who starves a child to death is possessed by the victim's spirit, which lodges in the body seeking revenge through the second mouth. The futakuchi-onna belongs to the same yokai category as rokurokubi, kuchisake-onna, and yama-uba — women afflicted with a curse or supernatural disease. It appears in the Ehon Hyaku Monogatari, an illustrated collection of ghost stories.

etymology.origin: Japanese (ふたくちおんな / 二口女)
etymology.meaning: Futa (二, two) + kuchi (口, mouth) + onna (女, woman) = 'two-mouthed woman'
etymology.first_use: Edo period Japan; appears in the Ehon Hyaku Monogatari (illustrated ghost story collection)

related_phenomena.name: Rokurokubi
related_phenomena.description: Japanese yokai of a woman with an elongated or detachable neck; same category of female body-horror yokai

related_phenomena.name: Kuchisake-onna
related_phenomena.description: Japanese yokai / urban legend of a slit-mouthed woman; shares the mouth-horror motif

related_phenomena.name: Yama-uba
related_phenomena.description: Japanese mountain hag yokai; same category of supernatural woman cursed or transformed

features.description: The woman appears completely normal from the front, with a conventional face and mouth

features.feature: Second mouth at back of head
features.description: A second fully functional mouth — with lips, teeth, and tongue — concealed beneath the hair at the back of the head
Hair moves independently, like a pair of serpents, enabling the second mouth to feed itself by grasping food with the hair

physical.morphology: Outwardly resembles a normal woman except for the concealed second mouth on the back of the head. The serpentine hair is the most distinctive behavioral feature, actively manipulating objects to feed the hidden mouth.

abilities.description: Hair moves like tentacles or serpents and can grasp, carry, and insert food into the second mouth
The second mouth causes excruciating pain to the host if it is not fed, screaming constantly until satiated
The second mouth mumbles spiteful and threatening things independently of the host

behavior.diet: Double the food consumed by a normal person — the second mouth demands and consumes food independently
patterns: Conceals the second mouth from others, particularly when eating
The second mouth screams when hungry, causing the host intense pain
The hair feeds the second mouth when the host is not actively eating

range.description: A creature of Japanese folklore; associated with rural Japan in the Edo period and earlier. No specific geographic region within Japan is particularly associated with the futakuchi-onna.

first_recorded.source: Ehon Hyaku Monogatari (illustrated collection of ghost stories, Edo period)

origins.cultural_roots: Japanese yokai tradition of the Edo period. The futakuchi-onna belongs to a category of female body-horror yokai that reflect anxieties about domestic roles, hunger, miserliness, and punishment for cruelty within the household.
origins.earliest_accounts: Appears in the Ehon Hyaku Monogatari, an illustrated Edo-period collection of ghost stories.
origins.folklore_connections: Related to the broad category of cursed or supernaturally transformed women in Japanese folklore. The punishment-through-curse narrative structure is common to many yokai origin stories. The hunger theme connects to other Japanese supernatural beings associated with starvation and the spirits of those who died from want.

timeline.event: Ehon Hyaku Monogatari published, containing an early illustrated account of the futakuchi-onna
timeline.significance: Earliest known illustrated documentation of the creature in print

history.modern_era: The futakuchi-onna has become a recognized figure in international popular culture through manga, anime, and video games drawing on the Edo-period yokai tradition systematized by researchers like Shigeru Mizuki.

paranormal.name: Vengeful spirit possession
paranormal.description: The spirit of a child or stepchild who was starved to death possesses the body of the cruel person responsible, creating the second mouth as an instrument of revenge that forces the host to consume food obsessively.
evidence_cited: The third origin narrative of the futakuchi-onna

skeptical.name: Moral allegory encoding domestic anxieties
skeptical.description: The futakuchi-onna encodes anxieties about domestic miserliness, cruelty to stepchildren, and the consequences of greed, expressed through a body-horror metaphor in which hidden gluttony or hidden cruelty is literally exposed.
evidence_cited: The creature's origin stories consistently involve punishment for stinginess or cruelty
The body-horror format (hidden mouth) represents the literalization of a hidden secret or sin

stories: A miser's wife eats little and develops a second mouth that demands twice the food, punishing the household's stinginess
A woman accidentally struck in the head by her husband's axe develops an incurable wound that becomes a second mouth
A stepmother who starved her stepchild is possessed by the child's vengeful spirit, which takes up residence as a second mouth at the back of her head
folklore.beliefs: The futakuchi-onna embodies the belief that cruelty and greed — particularly within the domestic sphere — will be punished through supernatural means. The hidden mouth represents hidden sins becoming inescapably manifest.

literature: Ehon Hyaku Monogatari — Edo-period illustrated ghost story collection containing the futakuchi-onna
film_tv: Various Japanese horror anthology productions featuring classic yokai
games: Appears in multiple Japanese video games drawing on yokai tradition
other: Featured in manga and anime works drawing on the Edo-period yokai canon

researchers.name: Shigeru Mizuki
researchers.affiliation: Independent manga artist and yokai researcher
researchers.contribution: Systematized and illustrated Edo-period yokai including the futakuchi-onna in his encyclopedic manga works on Japanese supernatural beings

research.ongoing_studies: The futakuchi-onna is studied as part of broader academic research into Edo-period yokai culture and Japanese attitudes toward the supernatural.
